# Public perception of autonomous vehicle capability determines judgment of blame and trust in road traffic accidents

![](https://orca.cardiff.ac.uk/164366/4.haspreviewThumbnailVersion/1-s2.0-S0965856423003075-main.pdf)

## Abstract

Road accidents involving autonomous vehicles (AVs) will not only introduce legal challenges over liability distribution but also generally diminish the public trust that may make itself manifested in slowing the initial adoption of the technology and call into question the continued adoption of the technology. Understanding the public’s reactions to such incidents, especially the way they differentiate from conventional vehicles, is vital for future policy-making and legislation, which will in turn shape the landscape of the autonomous vehicle industry. In this paper, intuitive judgments of blame and trust were investigated in simulated scenarios of road-traffic accidents involving either autonomous vehicles or human-driven vehicles. In an initial study, five of six scenarios showed more blame and less trust attributed to autonomous vehicles, despite the scenarios being identical in antecedents and consequences to those with a human driver. In one scenario this asymmetry was sharply reversed; an anomaly shown in a follow-up experiment to be dependent on the extent to which the incident was more likely to be foreseeable by the human driver. More generally these studies show—rather than being the result of a universal higher performance standard against autonomous vehicles—that blame and trust are shaped by stereotypical conceptions of the capabilities of machines versus humans applied in a context-specific way, which may or may not align with objectively derived state of affairs. These findings point to the necessity of regularly calibrating the public’s knowledge and expectation of autonomous vehicles through educational campaigns and legislative measures mandating user training and timely disclosure from car manufacturers/developers regarding their product capabilities.

# The public perception and adaptability of laws and regulations of autonomous driving vehicles

[*Humanities and Social Sciences Communications*](/palcomms)
**volume 12**, Article number: 1224 (2025)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

52 Accesses

7 Altmetric

[Metrics details](/articles/s41599-025-05600-6/metrics)

### Subjects

## Abstract

This study aims to explore the public’s attitude towards the development of autonomous vehicles and their perception of the adaptability of existing laws and regulations, and to analyze the interactive relationship between social acceptance and policy making. The study conducts questionnaire surveys to collect opinions from the public in specific regions, and combines legal text analysis to evaluate the responsiveness and flexibility of existing laws and regulations to autonomous driving technology. The study found that the public is cautiously optimistic about autonomous vehicles, but has obvious concerns about issues such as the definition of their legal responsibilities, safety assurance mechanisms, and privacy protection. At the same time, the current laws and regulations have obvious lags in technical standards, test access, and responsibility division, and it is difficult to fully meet the needs of the rapid development of autonomous driving technology. The important contribution of this study is that it systematically combines public opinions with legal adaptability assessments for the first time, providing empirical evidence for policymakers, emphasizing that while promoting technological development, the dynamic adjustment of laws and regulations and the establishment of public communication mechanisms should be promoted simultaneously, so as to build a social and institutional environment conducive to the healthy development of autonomous driving technology.

## Introduction

As a cutting-edge technology, autonomous driving technology is changing the transportation industry. The technology combines artificial intelligence, machine learning, and sensor fusion to enable vehicles to drive autonomously without human driver intervention (Garg et al., [2022](/articles/s41599-025-05600-6#ref-CR8 "Garg N, Ashrith KS, Parveen GS, Sai KG, Chintamaneni A, Hasan F (2022) Self-driving car to drive autonomously using image processing and deep learning. Int J Res Eng Sci Manag 5(1):125–132"); Omeiza et al., [2021](/articles/s41599-025-05600-6#ref-CR24 "Omeiza D, Webb H, Jirotka M, Kunze L (2021) Explanations in autonomous driving: a survey. IEEE Trans Intell Transp Syst 23(8):10142–10162")). It has played an important role in improving road traffic safety and reducing accident rates (Muhammad et al., [2020](/articles/s41599-025-05600-6#ref-CR22 "Muhammad K, Ullah A, Lloret J, Del Ser J, De Albuquerque VHC (2020) Deep learning for safe autonomous driving: current challenges and future directions. IEEE Trans Intell Transp Syst 22(7):4316–4336"); Pek et al., [2020](/articles/s41599-025-05600-6#ref-CR26 "Pek C, Manzinger S, Koschi M, Althoff M (2020) Using online verification to prevent autonomous vehicles from causing accidents. Nat Mach Intell 2(9):518–528")), which is conducive to promoting the efficient use of road resources, reducing energy consumption, and playing a positive role in alleviating traffic congestion and improving travel for the elderly (Grigorescu et al., [2020](/articles/s41599-025-05600-6#ref-CR11 "Grigorescu S, Trasnea B, Cocias T, Macesanu G (2020) A survey of deep learning techniques for autonomous driving. J field Robot 37(3):362–386"); Ma et al., [2020](/articles/s41599-025-05600-6#ref-CR21 "Ma Y, Wang Z, Yang H, Yang L (2020) Artificial intelligence applications in the development of autonomous vehicles: a survey. IEEE/CAA J Autom Sin 7(2):315–329")). With the advancement of technology, the potential of autonomous driving technology in commercial applications has gradually emerged (Hu et al., [2023](/articles/s41599-025-05600-6#ref-CR14 "Hu X, Li S, Huang T, Tang B, Huai R, Chen L (2023) How simulation helps autonomous driving: a survey of sim2real, digital twins, and parallel intelligence. IEEE Trans Intell Veh 9(1):593–612"); Liu et al., [2020](/articles/s41599-025-05600-6#ref-CR19 "Liu L, Lu S, Zhong R, Wu B, Yao Y, Zhang Q, Shi W (2020) Computing systems for autonomous driving: state of the art and challenges. IEEE Internet Things J 8(8):6469–6486")). In the process of promotion, the gap between the public’s cognitive differences about this technology and the lag of the current legal system has gradually become prominent. Whether the public’s trust and the legal system keep pace with this new technology have become the determining factors for its overall application. In the face of these factors, it is necessary to conduct extensive research to identify solutions to ensure that this technology can smoothly transition to wider social applications. Understanding public opinion and regulatory flexibility is crucial to promoting social acceptance of autonomous driving technology, policy formulation and implementation effect evaluation, and helps achieve the coordinated evolution of technological development and legal norms. The differences in public perceptions include trust in autonomous driving technology, risk perception, and willingness to use it, while the legal system involves regulatory flexibility and responsibility definition. Previous studies have focused on the technology itself, ignoring the interaction between legal adaptation and public psychological factors, resulting in insufficient understanding of its impact mechanism, which urgently needs in-depth discussion.

As a product of the deep integration of artificial intelligence and the transportation field, the development of autonomous driving technology has gradually moved from laboratory testing to commercialization. At present, major countries around the world are promoting the evolution of autonomous driving systems to L4/L5 through policy support and technical research. The speed of technological iteration far exceeds the pace of legal updates. The current “Road Traffic Safety Law” is mostly based on traditional motor vehicle management design, and lacks clear provisions on the identification of responsible entities, accident attribution mechanisms and data sovereignty of autonomous driving systems. Taking the European Union as an example, although its 2022 “Ethical Guidelines for Autonomous Driving” proposes the principle of “technical neutrality”, there are significant differences in legal adaptability among member states, resulting in compliance risks in cross-border operations. This structural contradiction between technological advancement and institutional lag has become a core bottleneck restricting the large-scale implementation of the industry. It is urgent to build a dynamic and inclusive legal framework to achieve a balance between technology governance and risk control.

Public cognition and social acceptance are another key variable in the promotion of autonomous driving technology, and its influencing factors show significant regional and cultural heterogeneity. Legal transparency is positively correlated with public trust: After the German “Autonomous Driving Act” requires vehicles to be equipped with “black boxes” and disclose accident responsibility determination standards, the public’s willingness to accept technology has increased. This cognitive-institutional interaction mechanism suggests that policy-making needs to take into account both technical education and rights protection - for example, through the “sandbox supervision” pilot to accumulate cases, establish a liability insurance classification system, and at the same time strengthen the explainable disclosure of algorithms to alleviate “technical black box” anxiety, and ultimately form a positive cycle of “technological maturity-legal improvement-public trust”.

In the context of autonomous driving technology research, there has been extensive debate in academia on social acceptance, ethics, safety, and legal and regulatory adaptability. Early studies mostly focused on technological breakthroughs and their difficulties, and believed that the cultivation of autonomous driving technology was premised on the parallel development of technological maturity and regulatory policies (Ahmed et al., [2022](/articles/s41599-025-05600-6#ref-CR1 "Ahmed HU, Huang Y, Lu P, Bridgelall R (2022) Technology developments and impacts of connected and autonomous vehicles: an overview. Smart Cities 5(1):382–404")). There are even detailed debates on the ethical challenges and responsibility allocation brought about by autonomous driving, especially during accidents, the complexity of the allocation of responsibilities between users and autonomous vehicles (Pöllänen et al., [2020](/articles/s41599-025-05600-6#ref-CR28 "Pöllänen E, Read GJ, lane BR, Thompson J, Salmon PM (2020) Who is to blame for crashes involving autonomous vehicles? Exploring blame attribution across the road transport system. Ergonomics 63(5):525–537"); Yazdanpanah et al., [2023](/articles/s41599-025-05600-6#ref-CR32 "Yazdanpanah V, Gerding EH, Stein S, Dastani M, Jonker CM, Norman TJ, Ramchurn SD (2023) Reasoning about responsibility in autonomous systems: challenges and opportunities. Ai Soc 38(4):1453–1464")). Public attitude research shows that personal trust and sense of security play an important role in the public’s acceptance of autonomous driving technology (Baccarella et al., [2021](/articles/s41599-025-05600-6#ref-CR3 "Baccarella CV, Wagner TF, Scheiner CW, Maier L, Voigt KI (2021) Investigating consumer acceptance of autonomous technologies: the case of self-driving automobiles. Eur J Innov Manag 24(4):1210–1232"); Schrauth et al., [2021](/articles/s41599-025-05600-6#ref-CR30 "Schrauth B, Funk W, Maier S, Kraetsch C (2021) The acceptance of conditionally automated cars from the perspective of different road user groups. Eur J Transp Infrastruct Res 21(4):81–103")). Although existing literature has touched on the importance of integrating technology ethics and law, how to strike a balance between public cognitive differences and legal adaptability, as well as the impact of government policy communication and social and cultural background on the social acceptance of technology, have not been fully analyzed. Therefore, in dealing with differences in legal and social cognition, current research needs to be further deepened.

In the field of research on the adaptability of autonomous driving technology and laws and regulations, some studies have used a variety of methods to conduct in-depth discussions. Through questionnaire surveys, the differences in the public’s perception of autonomous driving technology are analyzed, and the differences in the acceptance of this technology among different groups are explored (Othman, [2021](/articles/s41599-025-05600-6#ref-CR25 "Othman K (2021) Public acceptance and perception of autonomous vehicles: a comprehensive review. AI Ethics 1(3):355–387"); Yuen et al., [2021](/articles/s41599-025-05600-6#ref-CR34 "Yuen KF, Cai L, Qi G, Wang X (2021) Factors influencing autonomous vehicle adoption: An application of the technology acceptance model and innovation diffusion theory. Technol Anal Strateg Manag 33(5):505–519")). Structural equation model (SEM) is used to examine the degree of public trust in autonomous driving technology and the influencing factors behind it, revealing the issue of how legal responsibility is defined (Grotenhermen et al., [2020](/articles/s41599-025-05600-6#ref-CR12 "Grotenhermen JG, Gerdt SO, Schewe G (2020) Comparing customer perceptions of potential autonomous vehicle manufacturers: an analysis of the relationship between corporate reputation and intention to use. Int J Automot Technol Manag 20(4):408–435")). However, these studies only focus on single factors such as technological risk perception or legal responsibility allocation, and do not further explore how the degree of perfection of the legal system and technological ethics affect public cognition in the context of social acceptance. Public trust in autonomous driving technology is affected by technological safety and ethics (Giannaros et al., [2023](/articles/s41599-025-05600-6#ref-CR9 "Giannaros A, Karras A, Theodorakopoulos L, Karras C, Kranias P, Schizas N, Tsolis D (2023) Autonomous vehicles: Sophisticated attacks, safety issues, challenges, open topics, blockchain, and future directions. J Cybersecur Priv 3(3):493–543"); Gill, [2021](/articles/s41599-025-05600-6#ref-CR10 "Gill T (2021) Ethical dilemmas are really important to potential adopters of autonomous vehicles. Ethics Inf Technol 23(4):657–673")), but most studies still only involve one level of analysis. Other studies pay more attention to the complex interaction between legal system, social culture, and technological ethics (Bissell et al., [2020](/articles/s41599-025-05600-6#ref-CR4 "Bissell D, Birtchnell T, Elliott A, Hsu EL (2020) Autonomous automobilities: the social impacts of driverless vehicles. Curr Sociol 68(1):116–134"); Wang et al., [2020](/articles/s41599-025-05600-6#ref-CR31 "Wang H, Khajepour A, Cao D, Liu T (2020) Ethical decision making in autonomous vehicles: challenges and research progress. IEEE Intell Transp Syst Mag 14(1):6–17")), but fail to propose an effective theoretical model to explain the interaction between variables in real applications. Given that there are still few studies in this field, this study uses structural equation modeling and hierarchical regression analysis to explore the interaction between multidimensional variables such as public cognition, legal system legitimacy, technological ethics, and social and cultural forces. This study also reveals the complex and multipolar problems faced by autonomous driving technology in the process of popularization, and strives to build a complete theoretical framework to explore the coordination mechanism between public trust in autonomous driving technology and existing legal norms, and provide theoretical support for the construction of the rule of law based on technological innovation.

This study aims to build an integrated theoretical framework to explore the coordination mechanism between public trust in autonomous driving technology and existing legal norms, and to provide theoretical support for the formation of a legal system that adapts to technological progress. The study also examines the indirect effects of government policy propaganda, public cognitive differences, and legal awareness on promoting social acceptance, emphasizing the key role of the legal system in balancing technological trust and social acceptance. The innovation of this study lies in the fact that it combines structural equation modeling and hierarchical regression analysis for the first time, systematically reveals the interactive impact of public trust, legal adaptability and social and cultural risk tolerance, and constructs a multi-dimensional integrated theoretical framework, providing a new perspective and policy recommendations for the improvement of the legal system and social acceptance of autonomous driving technology.

## Theoretical background

### Theoretical basis

Public confidence in technology and the ability of the existing legal system to meet the needs of new technologies have become determinants of social acceptance (Lee & Kolodge, [2020](/articles/s41599-025-05600-6#ref-CR18 "Lee JD, Kolodge K (2020) Exploring trust in self-driving vehicles through text analysis. Hum factors 62(2):260–277")). The current legal system has deficiencies in dealing with ethical safety and responsibility definition issues (Copp et al., [2023](/articles/s41599-025-05600-6#ref-CR5 "Copp CJ, Cabell JJ, Kemmelmeier M (2023) Plenty of blame to go around: attributions of responsibility in a fatal autonomous vehicle accident. Curr Psychol 42(8):6752–6767"); Liu et al., [2021](/articles/s41599-025-05600-6#ref-CR20 "Liu P, Du M, Li T (2021) Psychological consequences of legal responsibility misattribution associated with automated vehicles. Ethics Inf Technol 23(4):763–776")), which has led to changes and uneasiness in public confidence in autonomous driving technology. The improvement of the legal system not only directly affects the formation of public trust in technology, but also regulates the interaction between social acceptance and technological trust.

There are significant differences in the public’s views on the risks of autonomous driving technology, which has a great impact on their views on the allocation of technical responsibilities. Risk perception is not only derived from technical cognition (Penmetsa et al., [2021](/articles/s41599-025-05600-6#ref-CR27 "Penmetsa P, Sheinidashtegol P, Musaev A, Adanu EK, Hudnall M (2021) Effects of the autonomous vehicle crashes on public perception of the technology. IATSS Res 45(4):485–492")), but also affected by the attribution of responsibility. When a vehicle drives itself and an accident occurs, responsibility becomes the primary issue (Kauppinen, [2021](/articles/s41599-025-05600-6#ref-CR17 "Kauppinen A (2021) Who should bear the risk when self‐driving vehicles crash? J Appl Philos 38(4):630–645")). Different people have different attitudes towards the allocation of responsibilities for ethical and legal issues of autonomous driving technology, depending on their risk perception tendencies, which is a major obstacle to the popularization of this technology.

In the process of promoting the full popularization of autonomous driving, the effectiveness of policy communication directly enhances people’s belief in technology, which in turn affects its acceptance (Ryan, [2020](/articles/s41599-025-05600-6#ref-CR29 "Ryan M (2020) The future of transportation: ethical, legal, social and economic impacts of self-driving vehicles in the year 2025. Sci Eng ethics 26(3):1185–1208")). Policy communication should not only focus on the public’s technical education, but also include the disclosure of legal rules and the allocation of responsibilities. To a certain extent, through the government’s active release of legal responsibilities and ethical safety knowledge related to autonomous driving technology, citizens’ legal cognition is also optimized, thereby enhancing their perception of technology. There is a close connection between the intensity of policy communication and the level of public cognition, and it indirectly affects the public’s attitude towards large-scale application by improving technical and legal cognition. Through policy propaganda, the government changes the public’s perception of technology, enhances trust in new technologies, and promotes the popularization of technology. The intensity of government policy communication also affects the public’s understanding of laws and regulations, further shaping their views on the application of technology.

Under different social and cultural backgrounds, the public’s acceptance level and risk acceptance of new technologies show differences. Risk acceptance in social culture not only shapes the public’s perception of technological risks, but also affects the dynamic relationship between technological ethical disputes and legal lags. Under different social and cultural backgrounds, the public’s acceptance of emerging technologies is closely linked to their attitudes towards related risks. In a cultural environment with high risk acceptance, the conflict between technological ethical disputes and legal lags may be alleviated, thereby promoting the rapid acceptance and promotion of technology. On the contrary, in a society with low risk acceptance, this conflict may be amplified, resulting in greater social obstacles to the widespread application of new technologies.

### Core variable definition

Risk perception is the public’s subjective assessment of the potential threats of autonomous driving technology. Its formation not only depends on technical cognition, but is also significantly affected by the tendency to attribute responsibility. When an accident occurs, individuals tend to attribute responsibility to technical defects, algorithmic decisions or human drivers. This attribution pattern will further strengthen their sensitivity to technical risks.

Technology acceptance is a comprehensive reflection of the public’s willingness to adopt autonomous driving technology. Its core driving forces include the synergy of trust building, legal protection and policy communication. According to the technology acceptance model, perceived usefulness and ease of use are basic dimensions, but ethical safety and legal coverage need to be considered in autonomous driving scenarios.

### Research hypothesis

According to the above background, the following four hypotheses are proposed in this paper:

H1: The public’s trust in autonomous driving technology and the coverage of the current legal system and ethical safety jointly affect social acceptance, and the legal perfection plays a positive regulatory role in the relationship between the two.

H2: The public’s perception of the risk types of autonomous driving affects their preference for responsibility allocation mechanisms through risk attribution tendencies.

H3: The intensity of government policy communication indirectly affects the willingness to scale up applications through the chain mediation effect of the public’s level of technology cognition and legal cognition clarity.

H4: Risk tolerance in social culture moderates the strength of the relationship between technological ethical disputes and legal lag.

## Methods

### Data collection

This study collects data using a questionnaire survey, which is aimed at the general public who have some knowledge of autonomous driving technology, and aimed to obtain data on the public’s attitudes toward the perception, trust, and legal adaptability of autonomous driving technology. The questionnaire design covers multiple dimensions, and strives to fully understand the public’s cognition of all aspects of autonomous driving technology, involving the advantages and risks of technology, the degree of perfection of laws and regulations, attitudes towards the popularization of technology, and concerns about ethical and safety issues.

This study takes China’s first-tier cities as a case study to deeply explore the differences in public perception of the adaptability of autonomous driving regulations. It combines the regional pace of technological advancement and regulatory response strategies to reveal the interactive relationship between regional legal evolution paths and social acceptance, providing empirical support and theoretical basis for international benchmark comparisons.

The participants in this study were adults aged 18 and above residing in various regions across China. A purposive sampling strategy was employed to ensure that respondents had basic awareness or exposure to autonomous driving technology. The questionnaire used the sample service of Wenjuanxing and was distributed through major online platforms in China to maximize geographic and demographic diversity. A total of 1061 valid responses were collected. While the survey was not restricted to a specific province, efforts were made to ensure regional balance in the sample.

In the regions covered by this study, policies and regulations related to autonomous vehicles are still in the initial exploration stage, the regulatory system is not yet sound, there is a lack of unified standards, and the regulatory mechanism is relatively backward. It is urgent to improve the legal norms in terms of technology access, road testing and liability identification to meet the needs of technological development.

The questionnaire survey of this study covers typical cities in North China, East China, Southwest China, and Southern China, including Shanghai, Nanjing, Hangzhou, Beijing, Chongqing, and Shenzhen. These cities were selected because they are highly representative in the research and development of autonomous driving technology and policy pilots. At the same time, they have diverse population structures and complex urban traffic conditions, which helps to fully reflect the public’s cognition and attitude towards autonomous vehicles.

The data was collected through an online questionnaire survey, which is published through various social platforms, online forums, and online channels of cooperative units. The questionnaire design fully considers the social impact of autonomous driving technology and its adaptability to laws and regulations, ensuring that the items cover the public’s trust, cognition, ethical risks, safety, technical functions, and laws and regulations on autonomous driving vehicles. The questionnaire is designed by autonomous driving technology experts and legal scholars to ensure the rigor and scientificity of its content. The respondents clearly inform their consent before filling out the questionnaire and confirm that their participation is voluntary. The questionnaire content involves the public’s personal information (such as gender, age, travel mode, etc.), understanding and attitude towards autonomous driving technology (such as views on the future development trend of autonomous driving, the advantages of technology, and ethical risks), and cognition of existing laws and regulations (such as the coverage of ethical issues by the law, the degree of perfection of autonomous driving regulations, etc.), as described in Table [1](/articles/s41599-025-05600-6#Tab1):

Table [1](/articles/s41599-025-05600-6#Tab1) indicates that 59.66% are male and 40.34% are female. The sample is mainly in the 18–30 age group, accounting for 47.5%. In terms of travel mode, 50.42% of the respondents say that they mainly drive; 35.25% use public transportation; the proportion of walking and non-motorized vehicles is relatively low, at 5.75% and 7.35%, respectively. This data reflects the potential differences in the cognition of autonomous driving technology among different groups, and provides a basis for analyzing the technology acceptance and legal adaptability.

The questionnaire is widely disseminated on major online platforms through Wenjuanxing, which is a popular online survey tool in China, and the exposure of the questionnaire is increased through Internet advertising and email push. After each participant completes the questionnaire, the system automatically records the time of answering the questionnaire and its source information (such as IP address, device type, browser, etc.). All participants must confirm that they have read and agreed to the informed consent form before they can continue to fill in the questionnaire. All questionnaires are equipped with multiple verification mechanisms to ensure the quality and accuracy of the data. Furthermore, if the participants do not complete the questionnaire or make obvious mistakes in the filling process (such as filling in too quickly or repeatedly selecting an option), their answers are excluded from the analysis to ensure the data validity.

The questionnaire survey is limited to the Wenjuanxing platform because they have high user activity and strong communication efficiency in the target area. However, the representativeness of inactive users is limited, and offline supplementary surveys will be considered in the future to increase coverage.

After the data collection is completed, the research team conducts strict data cleaning on the returned questionnaires. Firstly, invalid questionnaires (such as questionnaires with no answers or too short answer time) are eliminated. Secondly, possible extreme values or abnormal data are identified and processed to ensure the validity and representativeness of the data sample. After the data cleaning is completed, the effective sample size of the sample is 1061, covering a wider range of public groups.

The questionnaire after the preliminary design is reviewed by experts and verified by a small range of tr

SUMMARY:
Yet, while much research has been devoted to the factors of trustworthiness and acceptance of autonomous vehicles in everyday ordinary usage (e.g., Abe et al., 2015, Gkartzonikas and Gkritza, 2019, Gold et al., 2015, Hartwich et al., 2018, Nordhoff et al., 2019, Schaefer and Straub, 2016, Waytz et al., 2014), there is a dearth of research on how observers attribute blame in autonomous vehicle accidents along with its consequences for trust in autonomous systems more generally. Research in the area of human–machine interaction suggests that people treat computers as though they were social actors (Nass et al., 1994) and humans do attribute blame to autonomous systems (e.g., robots) in collaborative tasks (Furlough et al., 2019) which is a crucial factor of trust restoration after system failures (de Visser et al., 2018).

Public perception of autonomous vehicle capability determines judgment of blame and trust in road traffic accidents[☆](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#aep-article-footnote-id1)

Author links open overlay panel Qiyuan Zhang a c d e, Christopher D.Wallbridge b c, Dylan M.Jones a c d 1, Phillip L.Morgan a c d e f

Open access

Abstract
--------

Road accidents involving autonomous vehicles (AVs) will not only introduce legal challenges over liability distribution but also generally diminish the public trust that may make itself manifested in slowing the initial adoption of the technology and call into question the continued adoption of the technology. Understanding the public’s reactions to such incidents, especially the way they differentiate from conventional vehicles, is vital for future policy-making and legislation, which will in turn shape the landscape of the autonomous vehicle industry. In this paper, intuitive judgments of blame and trust were investigated in simulated scenarios of road-traffic accidents involving either autonomous vehicles or human-driven vehicles. In an initial study, five of six scenarios showed more blame and less trust attributed to autonomous vehicles, despite the scenarios being identical in antecedents and consequences to those with a human driver. In one scenario this asymmetry was sharply reversed; an anomaly shown in a follow-up experiment to be dependent on the extent to which the incident was more likely to be foreseeable by the human driver. More generally these studies show—rather than being the result of a universal higher performance standard against autonomous vehicles—that blame and trust are shaped by stereotypical conceptions of the capabilities of machines versus humans applied in a context-specific way, which may or may not align with objectively derived state of affairs. These findings point to the necessity of regularly calibrating the public’s knowledge and expectation of autonomous vehicles through educational campaigns and legislative measures mandating user training and timely [disclosure](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/disclosure "Learn more about disclosure from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") from car manufacturers/developers regarding their product capabilities.

*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003178)

Keywords
--------

Autonomous driving

Blame attribution

Liability

Trust in automation

Human-robot interaction

Artificial intelligence

1. Introduction
---------------

Even when all road vehicles are autonomous, there will still be accidents, some of which will result in considerable harm. Judgments of blame and liability will be shaped both by objective evidence and beliefs about the role played by the autonomous vehicle (AV) in determining the outcome, which may in turn be influenced by expectations of the operating capabilities of the autonomous agency —both specific and general—and how they contrast with those of a human operator ([Furlough et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0105), [Kim and Hinds, 2006](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0190), [Lee et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0220), [Lee and See, 2004](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0225), [Madhavan et al., 2006](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0250), [Muir, 1994](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0300)). Formal legal ascription of responsibility, mostly aided by expert opinions — e.g., accident investigators, legal professionals, among others, will have huge impacts on stakeholders (e.g., manufacturers, insurers, consumers, etc.) and perhaps even the longevity of the technology ([Bellet et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0030)). Informal human judgments of blame, on the other hand, such as those formed through first-hand experience or media reports, will be driven more by intuition and emotions ([Malle et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0255)). Nonetheless, non-expert opinions may have the power to feed into legal proceedings, e.g., through jury verdicts in courtrooms. They will also influence public trust ([de Visser et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0060), [Kim et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0195)) and in turn potentially determine the likelihood of autonomous vehicle adoption or continued use ([Adnan et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0010), [Choi and Ji, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0050), [Xu et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0440)). Understanding the characteristics of these judgments is therefore important to legislators and policy makers in ensuring the viability of autonomous vehicle technologies, as well as to citizens and the public who should form the majority of adopters.

Yet, while much research has been devoted to the factors of trustworthiness and acceptance of autonomous vehicles in everyday ordinary usage (e.g., [Abe et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0005), [Gkartzonikas and Gkritza, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0125), [Gold et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0130), [Hartwich et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0150), [Nordhoff et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0315), [Schaefer and Straub, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0400), [Waytz et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0435)), there is a dearth of research on how observers attribute blame in autonomous vehicle accidents along with its consequences for trust in autonomous systems more generally. Using textual and graphic vignettes describing traffic incidents and their antecedents, the work reported here examines how blame and trust are shaped by the knowledge of whether the vehicle is driven by a human driver (HD) or by an autonomous system (AS).

Legally, in many jurisdictions around the world, a human driver is expected to take full responsibility for a conventional vehicle while driving. For example, the Vienna Convention on Road Traffic (1968) stipulated that (human) drivers should retain control of the vehicle at all times and in most European states, (human) drivers of cars with assistive driving technologies are still required to monitor the system and can be charged with negligence in the event of an accident while the assistive system is in control. But this rule becomes progressively less plausible as vehicles become increasingly automated ([Anderson et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0015), [Gurney, 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0135), [Ilková and Ilka, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0165), [Księżak and Wojtczak, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0200), [Paret et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0335), [Pattinson et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0345)). For instance, in soon-to-be-realised Level-3 [automated vehicles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/automated-vehicle "Learn more about automated vehicles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), human drivers will no longer be required to actively engage in the driving task, assuming instead the role of a failsafe mechanism, intervening only at the behest of the autonomous vehicle’s computer ([SAE International, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0385)). However, in the case of a transition of control, it becomes problematic to pin-point the moment at which humans can reasonably be assumed to have full control of the vehicle and hence become legally responsible for the behaviour of the autonomous vehicle (see [Elish (2019)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0075) for a discussion on “moral crumple zones”). Nor are fully-autonomous vehicles without issues of responsibility. For instance, what level of competence should be expected of an autonomous system? How can responsibility be divided among the user, the manufacturer and the programmer who writes the AI algorithm? Clearly, obstacles involving responsibility and liability lie in the path of autonomous vehicle adoption ([Bellet et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0030), [Hancock, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0140), [Hancock et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0145)).

These difficulties are further exacerbated by the lack of explainability of the decision process of the autonomous system. While a human driver’s state of mind and physical capacity can be inferred from physical evidence and testimony, the autonomous system’s causal role in an accident may be particularly inscrutable, especially if its computing system is based on ‘deep learning’ ([Doshi-Velez and Kim, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0070), [Gilpin et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0120), [Ly and Akhloufi, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0240), [Zablocki et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0445)). At the same time, the causal chain of events as represented by the autonomous system’s actions may be so complex as to make a mechanistic attribution of blame infeasible, even for human experts. However, at least in the foreseeable future, due to the potential personal and social impacts of the tort cases relating to these accidents, the legal judgments will still be beyond the purview of artificial intelligence and primarily lie with humans (e.g., the jury, the judge, etc.). It follows that it is important to understand the human [cognitive processes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/cognitive-process "Learn more about cognitive processes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in formulating blame and understanding how this shapes trust in autonomous systems.

Blame is a [moral judgment](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/moral-judgment "Learn more about moral judgment from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") directed at an agent whose behaviours are deemed to have deviated from a norm ([Scanlon, 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0395)). It is not only based on an understanding of the agent’s contribution to a negative outcome but also on their reasoning, intentionality, obligation and capacity to prevent the outcome ([Malle et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0255)). The question as to whether inanimate objects like tools and machines are devoid of their own morality or intentions is still a topic of philosophical debate ([Hornborg, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0160), [Latour, 1993](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0210), [Latour, 1996](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0215)). It is argued here that a piece of technology could be at least infused with the morality and intentions of their makers/designers. Research in the area of human–machine interaction suggests that people treat computers as though they were social actors ([Nass et al., 1994](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0305)) and humans do attribute blame to autonomous systems (e.g., robots) in collaborative tasks ([Furlough et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0105)) which is a crucial factor of trust restoration after system failures ([de Visser et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0060)).

Trust is another psychological construct that is essential for human acceptance, adoption and continued use of automation systems ([J. D. Lee & See, 2004](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0225)). The role of trust has been examined extensively (e.g. within [social psychology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/social-psychology "Learn more about social psychology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and other disciplines) in the context of inter-personal relationships. It has been considered by many theorists of virtue ethics to be a virtue to ensure the flourish of human society which depends vitally on inter-personal collaboration ([D’Olimpio, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0055), [MacIntyre, 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0245), [Shionoya, 2001](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0415)). Evidence has shown that trust is a critical factor in organising economic activity such as commodity production ([Nyhan, 2000](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0320)) and market transactions (R. [Morgan & Hunt, 1994](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0290)). Despite its popularity in academic research, there is no consensus on the definition of trust due to its complex and multi-faceted nature. One of the most cited definitions was given by [Mayer et al (1995, p. 712)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0265): “willingness of a party to be vulnerable to the actions of another party based on the expectation that the other will perform a particular action important to the trustor, irrespective of the ability to monitor or control that party”. This definition was not developed for human interactions with automation and technologies but it denotes two important components of trust: the relinquish of control and the willingness to assume risk. This stays true when trust is discussed in a the context of human-automation interactions, where trust can be seen as the human operator’s intention to concede some or all of the control to an automated system while engaging in little or no supervision. By doing so, the human operator assumes the risk that the automation will not perform to the expected standard. It has been argued that the objective of an automation system design is to facilitate the _calibration_ of the operator trust – that is, to achieve a trust level that is proportionate to the capability of the automation system since under- or over-trust could lead to disuse, misuse and abuse of automation ([Lee and See, 2004](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0225), [Parasuraman and Riley, 1997](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0330), [Sheridan, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0410)).

Until now, human-centred research on autonomous vehicles has focused on ways in which systems can be designed, including ways they can be configured to promote their acceptance and trustworthiness (e.g., [Adnan et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0010), [Choi and Ji, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0050), [Forster et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0095), [Gkartzonikas and Gkritza, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0125), [Morgan et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0295), [Nastjuk et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0310), [Panagiotopoulos and Dimitrakopoulos, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0325), [Xu et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0440)) or the safety in handover between a human driver and autonomous system (e.g., [Eriksson and Stanton, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0080), [Lorenz et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0235), [Merat et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0280), [Merat and Jamson, 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0275), [Morgan et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0285)). While acknowledging its significant contribution to the development of [autonomous driving](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/autonomous-driving "Learn more about autonomous driving from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") technology—to its accessibility, usability and safety—this research was not intended to provide an understanding how blame is distributed in the event of accidents and how it may affect trust. [Lee et al (2021)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0220) examined the effect of automation failure on trust in automated vehicles but their investigation was restricted to scenarios where a malfunction or an overload of system capacity has resulted in a need for human take-over, not an accident. In the few other cases where accidents have been studied, the emphasis has been on [decision making](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/decision-making "Learn more about decision making from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") about accident antecedents, with the goal of understanding moral and ethical reasoning about autonomous vehicles, on what observers consider to be the most ethical decision once an accident becomes unavoidable — examples include the ‘trolley problem’ ([Awad et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0020), [Bonnefon et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0045), [Bonnefon et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0040), [Geisslinger et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0110), [Kallioinen et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0185)) — rather than on the attribution of blame and its effect on trust after the accident has taken place.

Research on blame attribution in which human drivers share the task of driving with an autonomous system reveals an inconsistent picture. Some studies show that the distribution of blame is proportionate to the perceived control that either party possesses over the outcome ([Bennett et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0035), [McManus and Rutchick, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0270), [Pöllänen et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0355)), human drivers receiving more blame than the autonomous system even though both parties contributed errors to the accident ([Awad et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0025)). Other work showed autonomous systems more to blame and responsible and their actions less acceptable, than that of their human driver counterpart when either the human driver or the autonomous system was depicted to be solely responsible for the crash of a semi-autonomous vehicle ([Liu & Du, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0230)). However, the discrepancies in blame attribution in this case might be the result of differing descriptions of the cause: For the autonomous system it was described as a ‘malfunction’, whereas in the human driver condition it was ‘inattentiveness’.

Studies of accidents with fully autonomous systems show that people apply inconsistent standards when making a judgment of blame on these systems compared to on human drivers.. A scenario in which both parties are partly at fault—a jaywalking [pedestrian](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/pedestrian "Learn more about pedestrian from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is hit by a car—showed that the blame on the driver of the car was higher if it was driven by an AI than by a human ([Hong, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0155)). The inclination to assign more blame to autonomous systems may reflect the general perception that autonomous systems should be more accomplished in driving than human drivers ([Penmetsa et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0350), [Schoettle and Sivak, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0405)). The study of human behaviour more generally has shown that the violation of norms or expectations (e.g., regarding a party’s capacity to prevent a negative event) is an important component of counterfactual thinking (e.g., “Someone could/should have done something”) as well as blame attribution ([Kahneman and Miller, 1986](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0180), [Malle et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0255), [Malle et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0260), [Roese and Olson, 1997](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0375), [Sanna and Turley, 1996](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0390)). Moreover, the repair of trust in a party after a breakdown in a human-to-human collaboration is determined by the extent to which the failure is attributable to that party (P. H. [Kim et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075#b0195)). That blame is associated with the violation of prior expectations of performance underpins the hypothesis that has guided the current work.

Human expectations possess the property of being complex and contingent. While machines are judged to surpass humans in speed and power, humans are believed to be superior in improvising, inductive thinking and judgments based on [prior experience](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/prior-experience "Learn more about prior experience from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([de Winter and Hancock, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856423003075

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: The public exhibits cautious optimism regarding autonomous vehicles (AVs), yet expresses concerns about safety, legal responsibilities, and ethical implications, impacting acceptance and adoption rates.

Trust in AV technology, perceived safety, transparency in legal frameworks, and effective policy communication significantly influence public acceptance, with cultural and regional differences also playing a role.
Educational campaigns are essential for calibrating public knowledge and expectations of AV capabilities, fostering trust, and mitigating fears associated with autonomous technology.
